h1: Illinois Property Tax Abatement Request Letter Generator
metaTitle: Illinois Property Tax Abatement Demand Letter Tool
metaDescription: Generate a professional Illinois property tax abatement request letter. Challenge your assessment under Illinois law with a clear, statute-backed demand letter.
deadline: 30 days from the date of publication of the Board of Review's assessment list
penalty: Refund of overpaid taxes plus statutory interest under 35 ILCS 200/23-20
introParagraph: If you own property in Illinois and believe your tax bill is too high, you have legal rights to challenge that assessment. Illinois law allows property owners to request an abatement or reduction of property taxes when assessments are inaccurate, when properties qualify for special exemptions, or when local taxing bodies have authority to grant relief. A well-crafted abatement request letter is often the first step before formal appeal to the Board of Review or the Illinois Property Tax Appeal Board (PTAB). Because Illinois operates with strict deadlines tied to publication dates that vary by township and county, acting quickly matters. This page explains how Illinois property tax abatement law works, what your letter should contain, and how to preserve your rights to further appeal if your initial request is denied.
lawExplanation: Illinois property taxes are governed by the Property Tax Code, 35 ILCS 200/1-1 et seq. Assessments are made at the township or county level, with most properties assessed at 33⅓% of fair market value (Cook County uses different classification ratios). When an owner believes the assessed value exceeds market value, is unequal compared to similar properties, or fails to reflect a qualifying exemption, the owner may pursue several remedies. First, an informal review with the local assessor can sometimes correct obvious errors. Second, a formal complaint may be filed with the County Board of Review under 35 ILCS 200/16-55, which must be filed within 30 days of the publication of the assessment list for that township. Third, taxing districts themselves have statutory authority under 35 ILCS 200/18-165 to abate taxes for specific qualifying properties, including new industrial development, affordable housing, historic properties, and properties in enterprise zones. Separately, 35 ILCS 200/23-5 allows refunds and abatements for taxes paid on assessments later found to be erroneous. Common grounds for abatement or reduction include: incorrect property characteristics (square footage, lot size, condition), uniformity violations where comparable properties carry lower assessments, recent arms-length sales below assessed market value, qualification for the homestead, senior citizen, senior freeze, disabled persons, or veterans exemptions, and clerical or mathematical errors. Cook County has its own procedures through the Cook County Assessor and Cook County Board of Review with separate filing windows by township. After exhausting Board of Review remedies, owners may appeal to PTAB within 30 days or file a tax objection complaint in circuit court.
letterStrategy: A property tax abatement request letter in Illinois works best when it functions as both a persuasive document and a procedural placeholder. Address the letter to the appropriate decision-maker: the township or county assessor for informal review, the County Board of Review for formal complaints, or the relevant taxing district board for statutory abatements under 35 ILCS 200/18-165. Identify the property by Property Index Number (PIN), legal description, and address. State the current assessed value, the value you believe is correct, and the resulting tax impact. Then build your case with evidence: recent appraisals, comparable sales within the last 12 to 24 months, photographs documenting condition issues, assessments of comparable neighboring properties showing uniformity violations, and proof of qualifying exemptions. Cite the specific statutory authority you rely on, whether 35 ILCS 200/16-55 for assessment complaints, 35 ILCS 200/15-170 et seq. for exemptions, or 35 ILCS 200/23-5 for refunds of erroneous assessments. Request a specific outcome: a revised assessed value, a refund of overpaid taxes with interest, or formal abatement. Set a reasonable response deadline and preserve your right to escalate to the Board of Review, PTAB, or circuit court. A clear, evidence-based letter often resolves matters without a formal hearing and creates a written record that supports later appeals if needed.
proceduralNotes: Filing deadlines in Illinois are jurisdictional and unforgiving. Board of Review complaints must be filed within 30 days of the publication of your township's assessment list, with publication dates varying widely. PTAB appeals must be filed within 30 days of the Board of Review's final decision. There is generally no filing fee at the Board of Review; PTAB charges a modest filing fee that varies by claimed reduction amount. Tax objection complaints in circuit court require payment of taxes under protest and have separate deadlines under 35 ILCS 200/23-10. Small claims court (Illinois limit $10,000) is generally not the proper forum for assessment disputes, though it may be used for related refund claims. Cook County has separate, shorter township-by-township windows.

faq.q: What is the deadline to file a property tax abatement or appeal in Illinois?
faq.a: You must file a complaint with the County Board of Review within 30 days of the date your township's assessment list is published. Publication dates vary by township and county, so check with your local assessor or Board of Review. Missing this deadline generally bars relief for that tax year, though limited exceptions exist for clerical errors under 35 ILCS 200/14-15 and for certificates of error in some counties.

faq.q: What is the difference between an abatement and an assessment appeal?
faq.a: An assessment appeal challenges the assessed value of your property, arguing it exceeds market value or violates uniformity. An abatement, under 35 ILCS 200/18-165, is when a taxing district voluntarily reduces taxes for qualifying properties such as new industrial development, affordable housing, or historic structures. Both can lower your tax bill, but they involve different legal grounds, decision-makers, and procedures. Many owners pursue both remedies in parallel when applicable.

faq.q: Can I get a refund if I already paid an inflated property tax bill?
faq.a: Yes. Under 35 ILCS 200/20-175 and 35 ILCS 200/23-5, Illinois allows refunds of taxes paid on erroneous or excessive assessments, generally with statutory interest. You may also pay taxes under protest and file a tax objection complaint in circuit court under 35 ILCS 200/23-10. Refund procedures vary by county, and strict deadlines apply, so document your payments and act promptly after any successful assessment reduction.

faq.q: Do I need a lawyer to file a property tax abatement request in Illinois?
faq.a: Individual homeowners can file Board of Review complaints and PTAB appeals without an attorney. However, Illinois law generally requires corporations, LLCs, and trusts to be represented by counsel in PTAB and circuit court proceedings. For complex commercial properties, large reductions, or circuit court tax objections, attorney representation is strongly advised. A well-prepared abatement request letter, however, is something most owners can draft and send themselves with proper guidance.

faq.q: What evidence should I include with my Illinois property tax abatement letter?
faq.a: Include your Property Index Number (PIN), current assessed value, your proposed value, and supporting documentation. Strong evidence includes a recent appraisal, comparable sales from the prior 12 to 24 months, assessment records of similar nearby properties showing uniformity issues, photographs of property defects or condition issues, and proof of any qualifying exemptions. For abatement requests under 35 ILCS 200/18-165, include documentation showing your property meets the statutory criteria, such as enterprise zone certification or affordable housing designation.
